What's going on here? Welcome to the six week challenge nutrition explanation. So whichever play you use, 42 day, six week challenge or any other place that we have in here, this is how the six week challenge nutrition works. So this one's a little bit different than the detox, all of them have a little bit of a different feel to them. This one's a little bit more flexible in that it's more like learning how to make it for yourself. So this also has four different plans instead of three. The reason one from four to three is a little bit easier. That's why we did three, but four has worked for five straight years, so it's not in any way an issue. Now the numbering system and how we have created, how you can allocate which plan goes to which type of person. We have a nice little numbering system here where if it says 1.75, then that would be anyone, let me actually break it down over here for you. So your portal may look different than this because we're constantly changing the look and feel of the portals, but I just want you to see this so you can understand it. So you have a 1.25, a 1.75, a 2.25, 2.75. So there's four meal plans here and then there's a vegetarian meal plan. The 1.25 meal plan is for anyone between the weight, like you can just pretend this decimal isn't there, between the weight of 100 and 150. And so 1.25, so 125 is the midpoint of that range. So these are 50 pound ranges of weight, so 1.75 would be anybody who's between 150 and 200. And then 2.25 is anyone who's between 200 and 250. So it's a really simple numbering system and I did it that way because my trainers understood literally it's like 2.25 or somebody who weighs between like 225, right? But we put the decimal there so that it wasn't as immediately apparent to our customers so that it just felt like a little bit more personalized. But at the end of the day, we just need to control their calorie intake. So let me dive into one of these. So I clicked this 1.75, which is probably one that you will use very frequently. Now, that is a rough estimate if you're out or you are in a rush, but we prefer that you actually measure this out. So the meals are these little plates here. And what you do is you pick one serving, one, and you pick any serving here from the corresponding column. And you go one serving blue, and you look at the blue column and you pick any one of these things to make your meal that is blue. Orange, go to the orange column, pick something that's orange. And they wanted a half an apple, so they'd look over here for their carb protein portion, and then it'd be four pieces of fruit. Very easy. Boom. We got that down for breakfast, and then vegetable. It says one cup spinach. We look over here, and we grab three pounds of some vegetables. And then that would be sufficient. Mind you, I want to be clear here. Vegetables, it says one cup, but we tell people to eat as many as they could. It used to say two cups plus, and then we'd have people complain about not being able to eat enough. And then if we have one cup, sometimes you were like, I wish I had more, and I'm wasting my vegetables. The point is, vegetables are flexible. If they want to have more vegetables, they can eat more vegetables. So that is one that you can clarify when you are making this, and you can give them the same explanation that I'm giving you right now. If they want to alternate, and have different, let's say they want to have only one breakfast, three days a week, and another breakfast, the other three days a week, all they do is cut the portions in half for their protein fat carbs and vegetable so that they would get half the amount, because they're getting half of the amount for that week. All you have to do is walk them through that process and then once you have explained the meal plan to them, then it should be straightforward. I wanted to bring this up because the 1.25 meal plan does not have, I don't know why the number says 0.55, it's just a 1.25 and it will by the time you see it. The fat column is empty. It is not that there is no fat in that person's diet. There is fat in everything. There's trace fat in carbs. There's trace fat in protein. There's trace fat in vegetables. Cell walls are made of fat. So it's just that we're not adding additional fat to the plan. They are still consuming fat and they will not have hormonal issues. There's a promise. We've done this a gazillion times. What happens if you decrease your dietary fat, where do you think the fat comes from? Their body. Then they start releasing fat from their fat stores to accommodate the fact that they don't have fat or as much in their diet. That is sort of the point of being in a deficit. So I just want to make sure that everyone's clear there. It's just that fat tends to be less, it's just less food than having sweet potatoes or rice, things like that. It's a little less filling. That is why this is structured the way it is. This is for the smallest type of person between 100 and 150 pounds. This will be typically somebody who might be on a body fat percentage diet. This is going to give them just enough carbohydrates so that they have energy during the workouts, but they are still going to be in a deficit, mind you, in the predominant amount of their calories are becoming from protein. I hope this makes sense. This should be a full explanation of how the challenge nutrition plan works and I have to explain that to them when you are with them in the consultation.
